title: Competitive Analysis — Systematic UX Benchmarking
content: A structured competitive analysis evaluates rival products from a UX perspective. Three competitor types: Direct (same problem, same audience), Indirect (same problem, different audience), Aspirational (best-in-class from adjacent domains). Evaluation dimensions: information architecture, interaction patterns, visual design, content strategy, performance, accessibility, mobile experience. For each key task, assess: support level, steps required, UX quality (1–5), unique approaches. Deliverables: summary overview, comparison matrix, individual competitor profiles, opportunity map, annotated screenshots. Focus on UX quality, not just feature presence — a poorly implemented feature is worse than no feature. Analyse full journeys, not isolated screens. Update regularly as competitors evolve. Include aspirational examples from outside the category to expand the team's reference frame.

title: Design Briefs — Defining the Problem Space
content: A design brief sets teams up for focused, effective work by defining the problem before the solution. Structure: 1) Project Overview — name, summary, business context, stakeholder. 2) Problem Statement — what, who, evidence, consequences of not solving. 3) Target Audience — primary/secondary users, characteristics, relevant personas. 4) Goals and Success Criteria — design goal, measurable metrics, qualitative indicators. 5) Scope and Constraints — in/out of scope, technical/brand/timeline/legal constraints. 6) Context and Inputs — existing research, competitive references, previous attempts. 7) Deliverables and Timeline — outputs, milestones, review points, deadline. Key principles: be concise but complete, focus on problem not predetermined solution, include measurable success criteria, get stakeholder sign-off before starting design. Reference the brief throughout the project to prevent scope creep.

title: Design Principles — Decision-Making Guardrails
content: Strong design principles guide teams through decisions and resolve trade-offs. Qualities of effective principles: Opinionated (takes a clear stance), Actionable (resolves debates), Memorable (short enough to recall), Distinctive (reflects this product's values, not generic), Testable (designs can be evaluated against it), Prioritised (rank order for when principles conflict). Structure each principle: title (3–6 words), statement, rationale, application example, counter-example, and trade-off acknowledged. Creation process: 1) Gather inputs (research, values, strategy). 2) Workshop to surface candidates. 3) Debate — ask 'Would anyone disagree with this?' If no one would, it's too generic. 4) Prioritise for conflicts. 5) Test against past decisions — would this principle have helped? 6) Socialise widely. Display principles in team spaces. Reference them in design reviews. Revisit as the product evolves.

title: Experience Maps — Systems-Level UX Visualisation
content: An experience map shows the entire ecosystem of user interactions across touchpoints, channels, and time — broader than a journey map. Horizontal axis (phases): awareness, evaluation, onboarding, regular use, advanced use, advocacy/departure. Vertical layers: User Actions (what they do, key decisions), Touchpoints (website, app, email, support, community), Channels (desktop, mobile, in-person, automated vs human), Emotions (confidence, frustrations, delight), Pain Points (friction, confusion, gaps), Opportunities (improvements, new touchpoints). Also map ecosystem relationships: how touchpoints connect, data flow between channels, human-to-automated handoffs. Use when: launching new products, evaluating omnichannel experiences, identifying ecosystem gaps, aligning cross-organisational teams. Always map current state before future state. Include both digital and physical touchpoints. Validate with research, not assumptions.

title: UX Metrics — Connecting Design to Measurable Outcomes
content: Meaningful UX metrics demonstrate design impact and connect decisions to outcomes. Four categories: Behavioural (task completion, time on task, error rate, feature adoption), Attitudinal (SUS, NPS, CSAT, perceived ease), Business (conversion, retention, support volume, onboarding completion), Engagement (DAU/MAU, session duration, feature discovery, return visits). Google's HEART framework: Happiness (satisfaction, ease ratings), Engagement (frequency, depth), Adoption (activation, feature uptake), Retention (return rate, churn), Task success (completion, time, errors). For each metric define: name, definition, measurement method, data source, target, frequency, owner. Best practices: choose 3–5 primary metrics, balance behavioural and attitudinal, set baselines before measuring change, connect metrics to design hypotheses, report alongside qualitative insights. Metrics without qualitative context are dangerous — they tell you what happened but not why.

title: North Star Vision — Inspiring Alignment Through Future State
content: A north-star vision is a compelling future state that inspires team alignment and guides trade-off decisions. Components: Vision Statement (who, what experience, why it matters — one sentence), Design Pillars (3–5 strategic focus areas defining differentiators), Vision Scenarios (concrete narrative stories of the future experience), Success Criteria (qualitative signals, quantitative metrics, milestones). Time horizons: near-term (1 year — tangible improvements), mid-term (2–3 years — major experience shifts), long-term (5+ years — aspirational transformation). Process: research synthesis, aspiration workshop, narrative writing, validation with stakeholders, communication plan. The vision must be inspiring but grounded in real user needs — not science fiction. Broad enough to accommodate unknowns. Used actively in reviews and planning — not a slide deck that gathers dust. Connected to daily work through pillars that inform every design decision.

title: Opportunity Frameworks — Prioritising Design Investments
content: Opportunity frameworks move teams from a list of possible improvements to a prioritised roadmap. Sources: research findings, analytics, competitive gaps, emerging technology, stakeholder requests, support channels. Evaluation frameworks: 1) Impact-Effort Matrix — 2×2 grid yielding quick wins, strategic bets, fill-ins, deprioritise. 2) RICE Scoring — Reach × Impact (1–3) × Confidence (%) ÷ Effort (person-weeks). 3) Kano Model — classify features as must-be (expected), one-dimensional (more is better), attractive (delighters), indifferent, or reverse. 4) Value vs Complexity — score user value (1–10) and implementation complexity (1–10). Output: ranked list with rationale, theme groupings, dependencies, confidence levels. Best practices: use multiple frameworks to triangulate, include diverse perspectives, revisit as you learn, document the 'why' behind every prioritisation decision — future you will need it.

title: Stakeholder Alignment — Navigating Influence and Decisions
content: Stakeholder alignment creates shared understanding of roles, decisions, and communication around design work. Key artifacts: Stakeholder Map (identify all stakeholders, map influence vs interest, categorise roles), RACI Matrix (Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed per decision type), Decision Framework (what needs input, who decides, how to resolve disagreements), Communication Plan (who/what/when, cadence, channels, feedback response timelines), Feedback Protocol (format, timing, prioritisation, conflict resolution). Common challenges: stakeholders designing solutions instead of defining problems, conflicting priorities without escalation path, late-stage scope changes, missing stakeholders discovered too late. Best practices: map stakeholders at project kickoff, establish decision rights before conflicts arise, communicate proactively (never surprise stakeholders), document decisions and rationale, revisit alignment as projects evolve and new stakeholders emerge.
